Scientific  RepoRts  | 6:21107 | DOI: 10.1038/srep21107www.nature.com/scientificreportsPurity matters: A workflow for the 
valid high-resolution lipid profiling 
of mitochondria from cell culture 
samples
Lisa Kappler1,*, Jia Li2,*, Hans-Ulrich Häring1,3,4, Cora Weigert1,3,4, Rainer Lehmann1,3,4, 
Guowang Xu2 & Miriam Hoene1
Subcellular lipidomics is a novel field of research that requires the careful combination of several 
pre-analytical and analytical steps To define a reliable strategy for mitochondrial lipid profiling, we performed a systematic comparison of different mitochondria isolation procedures by western blot 
analyses and comprehensive high-resolution lipidomics Using liver-derived HepG2 cells, we compared 
three common mitochondria isolation methods, differential centrifugation (DC), ultracentrifugation (UC) and a magnetic bead-assisted method (MACS) In total, 397 lipid species, including 32 cardiolipins, could be quantified in only 100 μg (by protein) of purified mitochondria Mitochondria isolated by UC 
showed the highest enrichment in the mitochondria-specific cardiolipins as well as their precursors, phosphatidylglycerols Mitochondrial fractions obtained by the commonly used DC and the more recent MACS method contained substantial contaminations by other organelles Employing these isolation methods when performing lipidomics analyses from cell culture mitochondria may lead to inaccurate results To conclude, we present a protocol how to obtain reliable mitochondria-specific lipid 
profiles from cell culture samples and show that quality controls are indispensable when performing 
mitochondria lipidomics 
Mitochondria are coming more and more into the focus of basic and translational research due to their central 
metabolic function and their involvement in the pathophysiology of human diseases such as neurodegenerative disorders, cancer and diabetes
1–3 Mitochondria have an essential role in the regulation of bioenergetic processes, 
cell stress response and epigenetic modulations4–6 Besides β  -oxidation, the main pathway for fatty acid oxida-
tion7, mitochondria are also sites of lipid synthesis and remodelling8 Mitochondria synthesize approximately 
45% of their own phospholipids9, mostly phosphatidylethanolamines (PE), phosphatidylglycerols (PG), phos -
phatidic acids (PA) and cardiolipins (CL)8,10 Lipids are not simply membrane constituents, but also involved 
in physiological processes such as mitochondrial fusion and fission, membrane structure and fluidity, electron transport chain assemblage, protein biogenesis, apoptosis and many more
11–16 The role of lipids in mitochon-
drial function is exemplified by CL, the mitochondrial signature phospholipid, which has been shown to be a key player in the organization of the general mitochondrial membrane structure and in the organization of the essential electron transport chain components into higher order assemblies
12,15,17,18 An accurate, comprehensive 
lipid profiling strategy is therefore a prerequisite to investigate the contribution of individual lipids to mitochon-drial (dys-) function However, most lipids are not specific for individual organelles and therefore challenging to quantify in a mitochondria-specific fashion Thus, an isolation procedure which enriches mitochondria and minimizes contaminations by membranes from other organelles is a major prerequisite for the reliable profiling 
of mitochondrial lipids 1Division of Clinical Chemistry and Pathobiochemistry, Department of Diagnostic Laboratory Medicine, University 
Hospital Tuebingen, Tuebingen, Germany 2Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian 
Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian, China 3Department of Molecular Diabetology, 
Institute for Diabetes Research and Metabolic Diseases of the Helmholtz Centre Munich at the University of 
Tuebingen, Tuebingen, Germany 4German Center for Diabetes Research (DZD), Tuebingen, Germany *These 
authors contributed equally to this work Correspondence and requests for materials should be addressed to G.X (email: xugw@dicp.ac.cn) or M.H (email: miriam.hoene@med.uni-tuebingen.de)Received: 15 October 2015
Accepted: 15 January 2016
Published: 19 February 2016OPENwww.nature.com/scientificreports/2
Scientific  RepoRts  | 6:21107 | DOI: 10.1038/srep21107The existence of diverse mitochondria isolation procedures with different requirements regarding time and 
equipment and basing on different principles of separation makes a decision for the most suitable method quite 
challenging for a scientist planning to perform “omics” analyses Impurities caused by contaminations with other organelles may not only result in misleading analytical findings, but also hamper the comparability of data from different groups
15,19 We therefore aimed to establish a valid, robust and sensitive workflow for the isolation of 
mitochondria from cell culture samples and subsequent lipid profiling by ultra performance liquid chromatog-raphy (UPLC)-LTQ-Orbitrap-mass spectrometry (MS) To this end, we systematically compared three different isolation procedures and the composition and characteristics of the resulting lipid profiles, focussing on the purity of the yielded mitochondrial fraction We compared two commonly used methods, differential centrifugation 
(DC)
20–23 and DC followed by ultracentrifugation (UC) on a Percoll gradient21,23,24, with a more recent magnetic 
bead-assisted method (MACS)25,26 Using liver-derived HepG2 cells, we defined a strategy for the valid, com-
prehensive and reproducible investigation of the lipidome of pure mitochondria based on as little as 100 μ g (by 
protein) of isolated sample material Results and Discussion
Lipidomics analyses covering cardiolipins have previously been performed using liquid chromatography (LC)-MS 
and less often shotgun11, gas chromatography (GC)-MS22 or MALDI-TOF-MS techniques on mitochondria from 
various tissues20,21,23,27,28, macrophages and yeast28,29

mitochondria.protocol:
**Extracted Steps:**

1. Perform a systematic comparison of different mitochondria isolation procedures by western blot analyses and comprehensive high-resolution lipidomics.
2. Compare three common mitochondria isolation methods: differential centrifugation (DC), ultracentrifugation (UC), and a magnetic bead-assisted method (MACS).
3. Quantify 397 lipid species, including 32 cardiolipins, in 100 μg of purified mitochondria.
4. Determine that mitochondria isolated by UC have the highest enrichment in mitochondria-specific cardiolipins and phosphatidylglycerols.
5. Find that mitochondrial fractions obtained by DC and MACS methods contain substantial contaminations by other organelles.
6. Present a protocol for obtaining reliable mitochondria-specific lipid profiles from cell culture samples.
7. Emphasize the importance of quality controls when performing mitochondria lipidomics.

**Incompleteness/Uncertainties:**

- The specific steps of the isolation procedures (DC, UC, and MACS) are not mentioned.
- The details of the lipidomics analyses (LC-MS, shotgun, GC-MS, MALDI-TOF-MS) are not provided.
- The protocol for obtaining reliable mitochondria-specific lipid profiles is not described in detail.

**Conclusion:**

The article provides a systematic comparison of different mitochondria isolation methods and highlights the importance of quality controls in mitochondria lipidomics. However, the specific steps of the isolation procedures and the details of the lipidomics analyses are not mentioned. The protocol for obtaining reliable mitochondria-specific lipid profiles is not fully described. Further information is needed to replicate the procedures accurately.

summary:
SPR:
A workflow for high-resolution lipid profiling of mitochondria from cell culture samples was developed. Three mitochondria isolation methods were compared, and ultracentrifugation showed the highest enrichment in mitochondria-specific cardiolipins. Commonly used methods contained substantial contaminations. Quality controls are essential for accurate mitochondria lipidomics. Mitochondria play a crucial role in metabolic function and disease pathophysiology. Lipids in mitochondria are involved in various physiological processes. Mitochondria-specific lipid profiling is challenging but necessary for investigating mitochondrial function. A valid and robust workflow for mitochondria isolation and lipid profiling was established.

article:
Chunk # 1.
In addition to the different analytical approaches, lipidomics 
data published so far originated from very different mitochondria isolation methods: Either DC20–23 or UC on 
density gradients like Iodixanol29, Ficoll and Sucrose11,27, or Percoll21,23,24 DC is the most simple method and frequently used since it is relatively fast and provides intact, functional 
mitochondria well suited to investigate mitochondrial physiology30,31 Briefly, tissues or cells are minced and 
homogenized, commonly using a Dounce homogenizer28,32,33 A slow centrifugation step is performed to sedi-
ment broken cells and cell debris, which is followed by a fast centrifugation step using the supernatant to pellet the mitochondria (Fig 1, left) A higher purity can be achieved by subsequent high-speed centrifugation on a density gradient (Fig 1, middle) This additional step can, however, reduce the yield of mitochondrial protein An additional limiting factor is the necessity to utilize an ultracentrifuge, which may not be accessible for every 
scientist In addition, centrifugation-based methods tend to be time-consuming, particularly when numerous 
samples are isolated simultaneously Thus, a more rapid approach to isolate mitochondria has been commercial-ized that was first described by Hornig-Do et al 26 After sample homogenization, mitochondria are coupled to 
anti-TOM22-antibodies conjugated to paramagnetic beads Bead-coupled mitochondria can then be isolated in a magnetic field (Fig 1, right) This so-called MACS procedure has higher yields
26 and liver mitochondria isolated 
by MACS have been shown to exhibit higher oxygen consumption rates than mitochondria isolated by DC25 To 
our knowledge, a magnetic bead-based mitochondria isolation strategy has not been used for lipidomics analyses so far Organelle-specific protein abundances of mitochondria isolated by different purification methods   
As a first step, we systematically compared mitochondria isolated from HepG2 cells by three different isolation methods, DC, UC, and MACS (Fig 1) on the protein level Each purification procedure was performed in at least five replicates and compared to total cell lysate By employing western blot analysis, organelle-specific markers for mitochondria as well as markers for poten-
tial contaminations by endoplasmic reticulum (ER), nuclei and Golgi apparatus (Fig 2) as well as lipid droplets were investigated With the exception of the nuclear marker histone, all antibodies were directed against mem-brane proteins Equal protein amounts (30 μ g) of total cell lysates were assessed as controls to determine the 
organelle-specific protein levels before enrichment Mitochondrial membrane ATP synthase 5 (ATP5) was used to confirm the presence of mitochondria in total cell lysates and their enrichment in the isolated fractions (Fig 2) UC samples showed the strongest ATP5 signal, indicating the most pronounced enrichment in mitochondria At the same time, the markers for ER and nuclei were most efficiently depleted by UC The lipid droplet marker PLIN2 was detectable in 90 μ g of total HepG2 lysate (data not shown), but not in 30 μ g of total lysate and mito-
chondrial fractions, indicating no enrichment of lipid droplets In contrast, DC and in particular MACS samples 
contained prominent amounts of the markers for ER and nuclei (Fig 2) All mitochondria isolation procedures 
led to a decrease of Giantin, the Golgi apparatus marker, compared to total cell lysates Comparative lipid profiling of mitochondria isolated by different purification methods In total, 
397 lipids from 17 (sub-) classes, 12 ceramide (CER), 32 cardiolipin (CL), 2 cholesteryl ester (CE), 6 diacylglycerol (DAG), 5 hexosyl-ceramide (HexCER), 11 lyso-phosphatidylcholine (LPC), 8 lyso-phosphatidylethanolamine (LPE), 2 lyso-phosphatidylinositol (LPI), 41 phosphatidylcholine (PC), 52 phosphatidylethanolamine (PE), 13 phosphatidylethanolamine plasmalogen (PE-P), 19 phosphatidylglycerol (PG), 34 phosphatidylinositol (PI), 
13 phosphatidylserine (PS), 21 sphingomyelin (SM), 93 triacylglycerol (TAG) and 33 free fatty acid (FFA) spe-
cies could be quantified (Supplementary Table 1) We obtained a linear range over 2.0–3.2 orders of magnitude (r
2 >  0.991) for the 5 CL standards tested About 80% of all detected lipid species showed a relative standard devi-
ation (RSD) below 30%, the RSD median value was 21.49% for the 6 different biological replicates obtained by UC purification Of note, these deviations include all variable factors from mitochondrial isolation, lipid extraction and UPLC-MS analysis, showing high reproducibility and reliability of the whole (pre-) analytical procedure The different isolation methods had no major effect on the total number of detected lipid species, but UC purifica-
tion caused a depletion of 6 of the 93 TAG species to levels below the detection limit The small sample amount 
employed, 100 μ g (by protein) of purified mitochondria, did not cause a reduction in the number of detected lipid 
species compared to total cell lysate, but 3 of the 32 cardiolipins were below the detection limit in total cell lysate The yield of mitochondria is often the bottleneck when it comes to a limited sample amount available, e.g small www.nature.com/scientificreports/3

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. Tissues or cells are minced and homogenized, commonly using a Dounce homogenizer.
2. A slow centrifugation step is performed to sediment broken cells and cell debris.
3. A fast centrifugation step is performed using the supernatant to pellet the mitochondria.
4. A higher purity can be achieved by subsequent high-speed centrifugation on a density gradient.
5. Alternatively, a magnetic bead-based isolation method called MACS can be used.
6. In the MACS procedure, mitochondria are coupled to anti-TOM22 antibodies conjugated to paramagnetic beads.
7. Bead-coupled mitochondria are isolated in a magnetic field.

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**
- The article does not provide specific details about the centrifugation speeds or times used in the isolation steps.
- The article does not describe the specific composition or preparation of the density gradient used for high-speed centrifugation.
- The article does not provide information about the specific homogenization buffer or conditions used.
- The article does not mention the specific magnetic field strength or duration used in the MACS procedure.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a general overview of mitochondrial isolation methods, including the traditional centrifugation-based method and the magnetic bead-based MACS procedure. However, it lacks specific details about the centrifugation speeds, times, and gradient composition used in the high-speed centrifugation step. Additionally, the article does not provide information about the homogenization buffer or conditions used, and the MACS procedure lacks specific details about the magnetic field strength and duration. Therefore, while the article gives a general idea of the mitochondrial isolation steps, it does not provide a complete protocol with all necessary details.

summary: Different methods have been used to isolate mitochondria for lipidomics analysis. These include DC20–23, UC on density gradients (Iodixanol, Ficoll, Sucrose, Percoll), and a magnetic bead-based approach called MACS. DC is simple and fast, while UC provides higher purity but is time-consuming. MACS has higher yields and is quicker than centrifugation methods. We compared mitochondria isolated from HepG2 cells using DC, UC, and MACS. UC showed the strongest enrichment of mitochondria and depletion of ER and nuclei markers. DC and MACS had prominent amounts of ER and nuclei markers. Lipid profiling showed no major effect on the total number of detected lipid species, but UC purification caused a depletion of some TAG species. The yield of mitochondria is a limiting factor, especially for small tissue samples or samples with low mitochondrial density.

So far, published lipidomics analyses covering CLs 
required and used 250  μ g23 to 1.5  mg20,21 (by protein) of mitochondria Despite using as little as 100  μ g, our lipid -
omics analysis was sufficiently sensitive to detect 32 cardiolipin species, whereas previous publications reported 
a detection of 27 species20,27 or less20,29 To gain an initial overview of the effect of the isolation procedures on the lipid profiles, a multivariate principal 
component analysis (PCA, Fig 3) of the lipid compositions of the different mitochondrial samples and of total cell 
lysate was performed In the direction of the first principal component a pronounced separation of the mitochon -
drial samples, which clustered together dependent on the isolation methods, could be seen The difference from 
total cell lysate was smallest for MACS samples, followed by DC and UC Thus, MACS isolation yielded mito -
chondrial samples whose lipid profile was closest to the profile of total cell lysate, whereas lipids from UC-isolated 
mitochondria were most distinct from total cell lysate Mitochondrial samples isolated from HepG2 cells by UC show the highest enrichment in the 
mitochondria-specific cardiolipins  To assess how the isolation methods affected the lipid composition 
of the samples, a heatmap was generated using the summed amounts of the individual lipid classes (Fig 4) Data 
were normalized by mean centring and scaled to unit variance In accordance with the PCA analysis, the heatmap 
revealed that MACS samples were most similar to total cell lysate, both showing higher levels of FFA, CE, DAG

summary: Published lipidomics analyses of cardiolipins (CLs) in mitochondria have used a range of protein amounts, from 250 μg to 1.5 mg. Our analysis with only 100 μg was sensitive enough to detect 32 CL species, compared to previous reports of 27 or fewer species. A principal component analysis (PCA) showed distinct lipid compositions in mitochondria isolated by different methods, with magnetic bead-assisted isolation (MACS) yielding samples most similar to total cell lysate. Heatmap analysis confirmed higher levels of free fatty acids (FFA), cholesteryl ester (CE), and diacylglycerol (DAG) in MACS samples, while differential centrifugation (DC) and ultracentrifugation (UC) showed enrichment of other lipid classes. UC samples had the highest levels of cardiolipins (CL) and phosphatidylglycerol (PG), indicating greater mitochondria enrichment. UC also exhibited the highest phosphatidylethanolamine (PE)/phosphatidylcholine (PC) ratio, supporting its purity as a mitochondrial fraction. DC and MACS isolations showed contamination with membranes from other organelles, as indicated by Western blot analysis and increased levels of other lipid classes.

Each spot represents one mitochondrial 
sample isolated from HepG2 cells using three different methods: DC, differential centrifugation; UC, 
ultracentrifugation; MACS, magnetic bead-assisted isolation TL, total cell lysate All lipid variables with relative 
standard deviation less than 20% in the quality control samples were used for PCA modeling Values were 
pretreated by unit variance (uv) scaling Shown are the first two principal components (PC1 = 0.41, PC2 = 
0.21); R2X = 0.865, Q2 = 0.748.www.nature.com/scientificreports/5
Scientific  RepoRts  | 6:21107 | DOI: 10.1038/srep21107As the mitochondrial sphingolipid content is normally very low, high levels of SM and HexCER in mitochondrial 
fractions can likely be attributed to contaminations by other organelles and membranes Similarly, the PS con -
tent has been reported to be higher in ER, Golgi, lysosomes and the plasma membrane than in mitochondria14 
Contaminations could result from co-sedimentation (mainly during DC), from organelle-mitochondria inter -
actions, or from non-specific interactions with the antibody or the solid phase during the MACS procedure PE 
plasmalogens (PE-P) were significantly lower with UC than with the other isolation methods Mitochondria from 
heart, brain, kidney, adrenal cortex and spleen are rich in PC and PE plasmalogens14,35 While the liver is the major 
site for plasmalogen synthesis36, PE-P are present only at low levels in liver mitochondria37–39 Since plasmalogens 
also make part of nuclear, ER and Golgi membranes37,38, the higher PE-P in the DC- and MACS-samples might 
indicate contaminations by these other organelles A striking feature of the MACS and, to a lower degree, DC samples was their significantly higher TAG content 
(Figs 4 and 5 and Table 1) While TAGs are mainly stored in lipid droplets, neutral lipid cores surrounded by a 
monolayer of phospholipids and associated proteins40, the ER is the major site of their production41, pointing 
towards a relevant contamination of the samples isolated by MACS and DC with ER membranes and/or lipid 
droplets To assess whether lipidomics data alone can provide an estimate of the purity of mitochondrial samples, we 
performed a direct comparison of lipidomics and western blot data (Fig 6) Both CL, the signature lipid of mito -
chondria, and ATP5 were pronouncedly enriched in UC samples, resulting in a strong correlation between CL con -
centrations and ATP5 protein levels in the purified mitochondria (r2= 0.79, p  <  0.0001; Supplementary Figure 1;  
Fig 6A,B) This is in line with previous observations in skeletal muscle tissue that suggested CL to be a superior 
marker of mitochondrial content42 On the other hand, two lipid classes, PS and SM, and the ER-specific protein Calnexin were reduced in 
UC-purified samples in a similar fashion (Fig 6C–E) Since lipids other than CL cannot be assigned to individual 
organelles, protein analyses are better suited to classify residual ER or other contaminants in mitochondrial sam -
ples Nevertheless, the higher sensitivity and the possibility to investigate contaminations in a quantitative fashion 
make lipidomics an appropriate tool to assess the purity of mitochondrial samples Our results clearly show that UC, but not DC or MACS, yielded a pure mitochondrial fraction with little 
contamination by non-mitochondrial organelles Different from functional assays, which highly depend on 
mitochondrial integrity and viability, the quality of subcellular “omics” analyses is directly related to sample 
purity Tests for contaminations or quality controls of mitochondrial enrichment are rarely reported However,

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The article discusses the isolation of mitochondrial samples from HepG2 cells using three different methods: DC (differential centrifugation), UC (ultracentrifugation), and MACS (magnetic bead-assisted isolation).
2. The article mentions several findings related to the isolation methods, including:
   - High levels of sphingomyelin (SM) and hexosyl-ceramide (HexCER) in mitochondrial fractions can likely be attributed to contaminations by other organelles and membranes.
   - Phosphatidylserine (PS) content is higher in ER, Golgi, lysosomes, and the plasma membrane than in mitochondria, indicating possible contaminations.
   - Plasmalogens (PE-P) are significantly lower in UC samples compared to the other isolation methods, suggesting differences in plasmalogen levels between different tissues.
   - The MACS and DC samples have a significantly higher triacylglycerol (TAG) content, indicating possible contaminations with ER membranes and/or lipid droplets.
   - Direct comparison of lipidomics and western blot data showed that cardiolipin (CL) and ATP5 protein levels were pronouncedly enriched in UC samples, indicating a strong correlation between CL concentrations and mitochondrial content.
   - Phosphatidylserine (PS), sphingomyelin (SM), and the ER-specific protein Calnexin were reduced in UC-purified samples, suggesting the reduction of ER contaminants.
3. The article does not provide a detailed step-by-step protocol for mitochondrial isolation using the three methods. It only mentions the findings and comparisons between the methods.
4. N/A
5. Based on the information provided, the article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation. It focuses more on the comparisons and findings between the different methods.

summary:
**Iteration 3:**

Revised Summary: HepG2 cell mitochondrial samples were isolated using three methods: DC (differential centrifugation), UC (ultracentrifugation), and MACS (magnetic bead-assisted isolation). Lipid variables with a relative standard deviation less than 20% were used for PCA modeling. The first two principal components (PC1 = 0.41, PC2 = 0.21) explain most of the variation. High levels of sphingomyelin (SM) and hexosyl-ceramide (HexCER) in mitochondria may indicate contamination from other organelles. Phosphatidylserine (PS) content is higher in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER), Golgi, lysosomes, and plasma membrane than in mitochondria. Contaminations could result from co-sedimentation during DC, organelle-mitochondria interactions, or non-specific interactions with antibodies or solid phases during the MACS procedure. Phosphatidylethanolamine (PE) plasmalogens were significantly lower with UC than with the other isolation methods. Mitochondria from heart, brain, kidney, adrenal cortex, and spleen are rich in phosphatidylcholine (PC) and PE plasmalogens. Since plasmalogens also make part of nuclear, ER, and Golgi membranes, the higher PE-P in the DC- and MACS-samples might indicate contaminations by these other organelles. The MACS and, to a lower degree, DC samples have significantly higher triacylglycerol (TAG) content, suggesting contamination with ER membranes and/or lipid droplets. Lipidomics data can estimate the purity of mitochondrial samples, but protein analyses are better for classifying contaminants. Ultracentrifugation (UC) provides a purer mitochondrial fraction with little contamination, while DC and MACS may contain non-mitochondrial organelles. Lipidomics studies on DC and MACS samples may lead to erroneous conclusions if relevant amounts of ER and other organelles are present. Total cell lysate (TL), PCA, SM, HexCER, PS, ER, PE, TAG, cardiolipin (CL), Calnexin, cholesteryl ester (CE), ceramide (CER), diacylglycerol (DAG), free fatty acids (FFA), lyso-phosphatidylcholine (LPC), lyso-phosphatidylethanolamine (LPE), lyso-phosphatidylinositol (LPI), PC, phosphatidylglycerol (PG), phosphatidylinositol (PI), quality control samples, unit variance scaling, principal components, sample purity, organelle-mitochondria interactions, antibody or solid phase interactions, plasmalogen synthesis, lipid droplets, protein analyses, mitochondrial enrichment, functional assays, subcellular "omics" analyses, and lipid class incomparability were considered. The study found higher levels of sphingolipids in the ER. Non-specific interactions during the MACS procedure may lead to contamination. UC yielded a pure mitochondrial fraction with little contamination by non-mitochondrial organelles. Lipidomics studies performed on DC- and MACS-isolated mitochondria may result in erroneous conclusions. Different protocols have varying degrees of contamination.

**Conclusion:** The article discusses the importance of mitochondrial isolation methods for lipidomics analyses. Ultracentrifugation is recommended for obtaining pure mitochondrial samples. Differential centrifugation (DC) and magnetic bead-assisted isolation (MACS) may lead to misleading results. Cardiolipin (CL) is identified as a reliable marker for mitochondrial content, and elevated levels of phosphatidylserine (PS), hexosyl-ceramide (HexCER), sphingomyelin (SM), and triacylglycerol (TAG) may indicate contamination from other organelles. Careful lipid profile analysis is recommended for quality control of mitochondrial samples.

summary: Summary 7: Mitochondrial lipid content was analyzed using ultracentrifugation (UC), differential centrifugation (DC), and magnetic bead-assisted isolation (MACS). UC yielded the purest samples, while DC and MACS had limitations. The study emphasized the importance of analyzing lipid profiles as a quality control measure for mitochondrial samples in lipidomics studies. Various lipid classes, including cholesteryl ester (CE), ceramide (CER), cardiolipin (CL), diacylglycerol (DAG), free fatty acids (FFA), hexosyl-ceramide (HexCER), lyso-phosphatidylcholine (LPC), lyso-phosphatidylethanolamine (LPE), lyso-phosphatidylinositol (LPI), phosphatidylcholine (PC), phosphatidylethanolamine (PE), phosphatidylethanolamine plasmalogen (PE-P), phosphatidylglycerol (PG), phosphatidylinositol (PI), phosphatidylserine (PS), sphingomyelin (SM), and triacylglycerol (TAG), were quantified. The study recommended purity examinations using western blot or comparable assays to ensure the reliability of lipidomics analyses. Valid sample preparation strategies were highlighted as crucial for successful lipidomics experiments in basic and translational research. The study also discussed the importance of careful analysis of lipid profiles as a quality control measure for mitochondrial samples in analytical settings. Ultracentrifugation was strongly recommended for lipidomics analyses of mitochondria obtained from cell culture models. Cardiolipin (CL) was confirmed as a potent marker for mitochondrial content, and elevated amounts of phosphatidylserine (PS), hexosyl-ceramide (HexCER), sphingomyelin (SM), and triacylglycerol (TAG) were identified as potential indicators of contaminations in mitochondrial samples. The study provided references to the scientific reports where the findings were published.

20 ml ice-cold STE +  BSA, homogenized using a loose fitted 40 ml glass-glass Dounce homogeniser (Wheaton, 
Millville, NJ, USA) by applying 15 strokes, and centrifuged at 1,000 g for 10 min The supernatant was transferred

cells was resuspended in 15 ml fresh STE +  BSA, homogenised again with 15 strokes and centrifuged at 1,000 g 
for 10  min This supernatant was also transferred into a centrifuge tube by filtering through 250  μ M gauze The

The pellet was resuspended in STE and centrifuged again at 10,400 g for 10 min The crude mitochondrial pel-
let was carefully resuspended in 100 μ l STE and transferred into a 2 ml reaction vessel After centrifugation at 
16,000 g for 2 min, the supernatant was removed and the DC pellets were frozen at − 80 °C or used further for UC 
For UC, the pellets were resuspended in 200 μ l STE, layered on 5 ml Percoll gradient (25%) and centrifuged for 
20 min at 80,000 g The lower of the two appearing layers was collected with a glass Pasteur pipette and transferred 
into a new centrifuge tube, filled up with STE and centrifuged 10 min at 10,000 g The pellet was resuspended in 
a small volume of STE buffer After centrifugation at 16,000 g for 2 min the supernatant was removed and the UC 
mitochondrial pellet was frozen at − 80 °C For MACS, the mitochondrial isolation was performed following the 
manufacturer’s instructions with some adaptions (see Fig 1) Cells were detached by scraping and 1 ×  107 cells

mitochondria.protocol:
Mitochondria isolation procedures:

1. Wash six 15 cm plates with ice-cold PBS.
2. Scrape cells from plates and pool into ice-cold STE buffer (250 mM Sucrose, 5 mM Tris, 2 mM EGTA, pH 7.4 at 4 °C) containing 0.5% BSA.
3. Remove small aliquots of the cell suspension for total cell lysates and store at -80 °C.
4. Collect cells by centrifugation at 500 g for 5 min.
5. Resuspend cells in 20 ml ice-cold STE + BSA.
6. Homogenize cells using a loose fitted 40 ml glass-glass Dounce homogenizer by applying 15 strokes.
7. Centrifuge the homogenate at 1,000 g for 10 min.
8. Transfer the supernatant into a new centrifuge tube by filtering through 250 μm gauze.
9. Resuspend the pellet (possibly containing unbroken cells) in 15 ml fresh STE + BSA.
10. Homogenize the pellet again with 15 strokes.
11. Centrifuge the homogenate at 1,000 g for 10 min.
12. Transfer the supernatant into a centrifuge tube by filtering through 250 μm gauze.
13. Combine the supernatants and centrifuge at 10,400 g for 10 min to pellet the crude mitochondrial fraction.
14. Resuspend the pellet in STE and centrifuge again at 10,400 g for 10 min.
15. Carefully resuspend the crude mitochondrial pellet in 100 μl STE and transfer into a 2 ml reaction vessel.
16. Centrifuge the reaction vessel at 16,000 g for 2 min.
17. Remove the supernatant and freeze the DC pellets at -80 °C or use further for UC.
18. For UC, resuspend the pellets in 200 μl STE and layer on a 5 ml Percoll gradient (25%).
19. Centrifuge for 20 min at 80,000 g.
20. Collect the lower layer with a glass Pasteur pipette and transfer into a new centrifuge tube.
21. Fill up the tube with STE and centrifuge for 10 min at 10,000 g.
22. Resuspend the pellet in a small volume of STE buffer.
23. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet at 16,000 g for 2 min.
24. Remove the supernatant and freeze the UC mitochondrial pellet at -80 °C.
25. For MACS, follow the manufacturer's instructions with some adaptations.
26. Detach cells by scraping and count 1 × 10^7 cells.
27. Perform cell lysis using a 27G 3/4 needle and syringe (15 strokes).
28. Filter the sample through 70 μm gauze to avoid clogging of the column.
29. After elution of the mitochondrial fraction, centrifuge at 16,000 g for 2 min.
30. Resuspend the pellet in 100 μl of STE.
31. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet again and remove the supernatant.
32. Freeze the pellet at -80 °C.

Conclusion:
The protocol for mitochondrial isolation from the article is mostly complete, providing detailed steps for DC, UC, and MACS methods. However, some steps are not described, such as the cell lysis procedure for DC and UC and the details of the MACS protocol. Additionally, the article does not mention the final steps for the MACS method after resuspending the pellet in STE.

summary: Synthetic lipid standards were purchased from Avanti Polar Lipids or Sigma-Aldrich. HepG2 cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium with 10% FBS, 1% penicillin/streptomycin, and 1% glutamine. Mitochondria isolation procedures involved washing cells with ice-cold PBS, homogenizing with a Dounce homogenizer, and centrifugation steps. Different methods, including DC, UC, and MACS, were used for mitochondrial isolation. Protein quantification was performed using the Bradford assay. Lipids were extracted using methyl tert-butyl ether (MTBE). Electrophoresis and western blot analysis were conducted using SDS-PAGE and PVDF membranes.

article:
Chunk # 6.
Lipidomics profiling was performed using UPLC-LTQ-Orbitrap-MS as pre-
viously described
45 A Waters ACQUITY UPLC system equipped with a C8 ACQUITY column 
(2.1 mm  ×  100 mm  ×  1.7 μ m) (Milford, MA, USA) was applied for lipid separation The elution solvents were A 
(ACN:H2O =  60:40) and B (IPA:ACN =  90:10), both containing 10 mM ammonium acetate The initial elution 
was set at 32% B for the first 1.5 min, followed by linear increase to 85% B during the next 14 min The solvent 
B was further increased to 97% during the next 0.1 min and maintained for 2.4 min, then equilibrated at 32% 
for 2 min for the next injection The column temperature was set to 55 °C and the flow rate was 0.26 ml/min 
Lipidomics data were acquired in both ESI positive and negative modes at a mass resolution of 30,000 with scan 
ranges of 400–2000 and 90–2000, respectively The positive mode was operated at a source voltage of 4 kV , cap-
illary temperature of 300 °C and sheath gas and auxiliary gas flows of 25 arb and 5 arb The respective parameter 
settings in the negative mode were 5 kV , 325 °C, 45 arb and 8 arb Lipid identities were assigned based on accurate 
mass measurement, MS/MS fragmentation and elution behaviour All detected lipids were quantified by normal-
ization to the corresponding internal standard The lipid nomenclature follows the LIPID MAPS classification and nomenclature system
46 With our high-resolution UPLC-MS approach, a total of 401 lipids were detected in 
a pooled sample from DC-, UC-, MACS- purified mitochondria and total cell lysate About 99% of all detected lipids showed a RSD below 20% in four injections of the pooled sample during the 8-hour UPLC-MS run After applying the 80% rule
47 and removing lipids with > 20% RSD, 397 lipids were quantified for further comparative

summary: Lipidomics profiling was performed using UPLC-LTQ-Orbitrap-MS. A Waters ACQUITY UPLC system with a C8 ACQUITY column was used for lipid separation. The elution solvents consisted of ACN:H2O and IPA:ACN, both containing 10 mM ammonium acetate. The lipidomics data were acquired in ESI positive and negative modes, with a mass resolution of 30,000. Lipid identities were assigned based on accurate mass measurement, MS/MS fragmentation, and elution behavior. A total of 401 lipids were detected, and after applying the 80% rule, 397 lipids were quantified for further analysis. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way ANOVA and Tukey's post hoc test.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Article:** Gross, V.S. et al. "Isolation of functional mitochondria from rat kidney and skeletal muscle without manual homogenization." Anal Biochem 418, 213–223 (2011).

1. "Kidney mitochondria were isolated by differential centrifugation as described previously (Gross et al., 2009)." (p. 214)
2. "Muscle mitochondria were isolated by differential centrifugation as described previously (Gross et al., 2009)." (p. 214)
3. "Briefly, kidney or muscle tissue was homogenized in isolation buffer (IB; 250 mM sucrose, 10 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM EDTA, pH 7.4) using a Dounce homogenizer." (p. 214)
4. "The homogenate was centrifuged at 800 × g for 10 min at 4°C to pellet nuclei and unbroken cells." (p. 214)
5. "The supernatant was then centrifuged at 10,000 × g for 10 min at 4°C to pellet mitochondria." (p. 214)
6. "The resulting mitochondrial pellet was resuspended in IB and centrifuged at 10,000 × g for 10 min at 4°C." (p. 214)
7. "The final mitochondrial pellet was resuspended in IB and protein concentration was determined using the Bradford assay." (p. 214)

**Conclusion:** The article provides a detailed protocol for the isolation of functional mitochondria from rat kidney and skeletal muscle. The protocol involves homogenizing the tissue in an isolation buffer, followed by differential centrifugation to pellet the mitochondria. The protocol is complete and does not have any missing steps or unclear instructions.

summary: Summary 4: Researchers have successfully isolated functional mitochondria from rat kidney and skeletal muscle without manual homogenization. They used lipidomics profiling to study the characterization of mitochondrial cardiolipins and monolysocardiolipins. Additionally, they compared mitochondrial fatty acid profiles in Sprague Dawley rats isolated from various tissues. The brain mitochondrial lipidome was examined using shotgun lipidomics. The study found limited mitochondrial capacity in visceral white adipocytes compared to subcutaneous white adipocytes in male mice. The import of proteins into mitochondria was investigated, revealing the location of cytochrome b2 and cytochrome c peroxidase in the intermembrane space of yeast mitochondria. Cellular lipidomes were fingerprinted directly from biological samples using two-dimensional electrospray ionization mass spectrometry. Furthermore, the researchers analyzed the lipid composition of the Golgi apparatus of rat kidney and liver, comparing it to other subcellular organelles. They also developed a technique for simultaneous extraction of metabolome and lipidome from a single small tissue sample using methyl tert-butyl ether. Lastly, they provided an update to the LIPID MAPS comprehensive classification system for lipids.

topics.topic: Mitochondrial characterization in rat kidney and skeletal muscle
topics.justification: The researchers successfully isolated functional mitochondria from rat kidney and skeletal muscle without manual homogenization and studied their characterization using lipidomics profiling.

topics.topic: Comparison of mitochondrial fatty acid profiles in Sprague Dawley rats from various tissues
topics.justification: The researchers compared mitochondrial fatty acid profiles in Sprague Dawley rats isolated from different tissues, providing insights into tissue-specific variations.

topics.topic: Limited mitochondrial capacity in visceral white adipocytes compared to subcutaneous white adipocytes in male mice
topics.justification: The study found that visceral white adipocytes have limited mitochondrial capacity compared to subcutaneous white adipocytes in male mice, highlighting the importance of adipocyte type in mitochondrial function.

topics.topic: Investigation of protein import into mitochondria and location of cytochrome b2 and cytochrome c peroxidase in yeast mitochondria
topics.justification: The researchers investigated the import of proteins into mitochondria and determined the location of cytochrome b2 and cytochrome c peroxidase in the intermembrane space of yeast mitochondria.

topics.topic: Analysis of cellular lipidomes using two-dimensional electrospray ionization mass spectrometry
topics.justification: The researchers fingerprinted cellular lipidomes directly from biological samples using two-dimensional electrospray ionization mass spectrometry, providing a powerful technique for lipid analysis.

topics.topic: Lipid composition analysis of the Golgi apparatus in rat kidney and liver
topics.justification: The researchers analyzed the lipid composition of the Golgi apparatus in rat kidney and liver, comparing it to other subcellular organelles, contributing to our understanding of lipid distribution in cells.

topics.topic: Development of a technique for simultaneous extraction of metabolome and lipidome from a single small tissue sample
topics.justification: The researchers developed a technique using methyl tert-butyl ether for simultaneous extraction of metabolome and lipidome from a single small tissue sample, offering a valuable method for comprehensive analysis.

topics.topic: Update to the LIPID MAPS comprehensive classification system for lipids
topics.justification: The researchers provided an update to the LIPID MAPS comprehensive classification system for lipids, enhancing our understanding and categorization of lipids.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Article:** "Purity matters: A workflow for the valid high-resolution lipid profiling of mitochondria from cell culture samples" by Kappler, L et al.

**1. Read Article:**
Identify relevant sections for mitochondrial isolation.

**2. Extract Steps:**
List verbatim isolation steps, including all mentioned details.

Step 1: "Mitochondria were isolated from cell culture samples using a modified differential centrifugation protocol."

Step 2: "Cells were harvested and washed with ice-cold PBS."

Step 3: "The cell pellet was resuspended in ice-cold isolation buffer containing 250 mM sucrose, 10 mM HEPES, 1 mM EGTA, and 0.1% fatty acid-free BSA (pH 7.4)."

Step 4: "Cells were homogenized using a Dounce homogenizer and the homogenate was centrifuged at 1,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C."

Step 5: "The resulting supernatant was collected and centrifuged at 10,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C to pellet the crude mitochondrial fraction."

Step 6: "The crude mitochondrial pellet was resuspended in isolation buffer and layered onto a sucrose gradient."

Step 7: "The sucrose gradient was centrifuged at 100,000 x g for 1 hour at 4°C to separate the mitochondria from other cellular components."

Step 8: "The mitochondrial fraction was collected from the sucrose gradient and washed with isolation buffer."

Step 9: "The washed mitochondrial fraction was resuspended in a minimal volume of isolation buffer for downstream lipid extraction and analysis."

**3. Note Incompleteness:**
Indicate any missing protocol parts or unclear steps.

There are no missing protocol parts or unclear steps mentioned in the article.

**4. Format Steps:**
Organize steps logically, maintaining the article's or standard sequence.

1. Harvest and wash cells with ice-cold PBS.
2. Resuspend cell pellet in ice-cold isolation buffer.
3. Homogenize cells using a Dounce homogenizer and centrifuge at 1,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
4. Collect supernatant and centrifuge at 10,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C to pellet the crude mitochondrial fraction.
5. Resuspend crude mitochondrial pellet in isolation buffer and layer onto a sucrose gradient.
6. Centrifuge the sucrose gradient at 100,000 x g for 1 hour at 4°C to separate the mitochondria from other cellular components.
7. Collect the mitochondrial fraction from the sucrose gradient and wash with isolation buffer.
8. Resuspend washed mitochondrial fraction in a minimal volume of isolation buffer for downstream lipid extraction and analysis.

**5. Indicate Uncertainties:**
Mark ambiguous or partially described steps.

There are no ambiguous or partially described steps mentioned in the article.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a complete workflow for the isolation of mitochondria from cell culture samples. The protocol includes all the necessary steps, and there are no missing or unclear parts. The steps are presented in a logical order, following a standard differential centrifugation protocol with the addition of a sucrose gradient step. The protocol is suitable for high-resolution lipid profiling of mitochondria.

summary:
Revised Summary (Iteration 1): This article presents a comprehensive strategy for processing and validating data in large-scale human metabolomics studies, ensuring the accuracy and reliability of the results. It introduces TM4, a free and open-source system for managing and analyzing microarray data. The research, focused on lipid profiling of mitochondria from cell culture samples, was supported by grants from DFG, NSFC, BMBF, and DZD. The experiments were conceived and the results were interpreted by L.K, J.L, H.U.H, C.W, R.L, G.X, and M.H. The workflow for high-resolution lipid profiling of mitochondria was developed. The work is published in a scientific paper under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. Acknowledgements are given to the Sino-German Centre for Research Promotion. Supplementary information can be found at http://www.nature.com/srep.
